situation: A two-sided marketplace for short-term rentals with cold supply (a few NYC listings) and almost no demand. The site looked unprofessional, photos were bad, and the founders were running out of money. Marketplaces are notoriously hard to start because supply and demand have to come up together.
action: Brian Chesky and Joe Gebbia flew to New York every other week, knocked on every host's door, took 'professional' photos themselves, helped hosts rewrite their listings, and listened to complaints. They also funded the company by selling Obama O's and Cap'n McCain's cereal boxes during the 2008 election to bridge to the next round. Later they used a Craigslist cross-post hack to source demand from a much larger marketplace.
result: Within weeks, listings with the new photos were earning 2x. The unscalable door-to-door period made the difference between Airbnb dying and Airbnb finding traction; they got into YC W09 and grew into a juggernaut.
lesson: Marketplaces are fragile early. Founders must do heroic, unscalable supply-side work — go to where the supply is and fix it by hand — until usage forces the work to be scaled.
source: Paul Graham, 'Do Things that Don't Scale'

situation: Stripe was solving an urgent problem (developer-friendly payments) but the incumbent path was 'wait for users to come to a clean API'. Most YC startups would have asked 'will you try our beta?' and emailed a link.
action: Patrick and John Collison did the opposite. When anyone agreed to try Stripe, they would say 'Right then, give me your laptop' and integrate it on the spot. They installed Stripe for users personally, in person.
result: Stripe became famous within YC for aggressive, in-person early user acquisition. It compounded into a developer-default payments company because the founders refused to wait.
lesson: Founder-driven, in-person integration beats 'we'll send you a link'. Compress the time between user-says-yes and user-uses-product to zero.
source: Paul Graham, 'Do Things that Don't Scale'

situation: Stanford students building a delivery startup with no app, no drivers, no restaurant deals. They wanted to test whether local restaurants needed a delivery layer at all before writing serious code.
action: They put up a one-page site called 'Palo Alto Delivery' with PDFs of restaurant menus and a phone number. When orders came in, they delivered the food themselves in their own cars. No fleet, no driver app, no backend.
result: Demand exceeded what they could handle within weeks. Only after that signal did they build a real product, raise money, and join YC. The manual-ops period told them what was real before they built.
lesson: Test the demand side with a fake or manual product before you build anything. Founders should do the work themselves first — the friction tells you what to build.
source: Stanley Tang & Tony Xu, 'How DoorDash Started' (Stanford CS183B / Startup School lectures)

situation: Drew Houston had a working file-sync prototype but no easy way to demo it. Investors and users couldn't tell the difference between his product and the dozen other 'sync to the cloud' tools that didn't actually work. He needed demand evidence before scaling distribution.
action: He made a 3-minute explainer video showing the product in use, with tongue-in-cheek references aimed at Hacker News. He posted it on Hacker News and Digg as a beta signup gate.
result: The waitlist went from 5,000 to 75,000 overnight. The signal was unambiguous: people wanted this, and the founders could now justify going deep on the product.
lesson: When the product is hard to demo, the MVP can be a video, not the product. The point of an MVP is to compress the cost of validating demand, not to ship code.
source: Drew Houston interview, Founders at Work / YC Library

situation: A community/marketplace for links has a brutal cold-start problem: empty sites stay empty because nobody posts to a site with no posts. Reddit launched with no users and no content.
action: Steve Huffman and Alexis Ohanian created dozens of fake user accounts and posted the early content themselves to make the site look populated and to seed the kind of links they wanted to see. They also ran the moderation and curation manually.
result: Real users arrived to what looked like an active community. The seeded content set the tone (tech, programming, quirky humor) that defined Reddit's early identity. Within months, organic users were posting more than the founders.
lesson: Two-sided communities die from cold-start. Founders must seed the supply side themselves — even if they have to fake it — until real supply arrives.
source: Steve Huffman & Alexis Ohanian, multiple Startup School / interview retellings

situation: Apoorva Mehta wanted to build same-hour grocery delivery. Earlier attempts at this (Webvan, Kozmo) had failed expensively. He had no shoppers, no driver fleet, no app, no warehouse — and a thesis that previous attempts had failed by scaling supply before validating demand.
action: He built a basic app and personally went to the supermarket, shopped the orders, and drove the groceries to the customer's door himself. He was the entire supply side for the first weeks.
result: Demand grew faster than he could deliver alone. Only at that point did he hire shoppers and raise money. Instacart became one of the largest grocery-delivery platforms.
lesson: When previous attempts at your idea died from premature scale, do the unscalable yourself first. Demand has to outrun your hands before you build the system.
source: Apoorva Mehta interview, How I Built This / YC Library

situation: Henrique Dubugras and Pedro Franceschi entered YC working on a VR startup. Within weeks of starting they noticed they could not even get a corporate credit card themselves as foreign founders without US credit history.
action: They abandoned the VR idea, talked to other YC founders about the same pain, and within their YC batch pivoted to a corporate card targeted at startups. They used their own pain as the wedge — a single, specific, painful job.
result: Brex grew to a multi-billion-dollar valuation in a few years and became default for early-stage YC companies. The pivot off a 'made-up' idea onto an organic pain point made the company.
lesson: Made-up ideas (VR for X) sound plausible but fail. Organic ideas — the founder's own pain — are the ones that have demand baked in. Pivot fast when you notice you're working on the made-up one.
source: Henrique Dubugras, multiple YC / Startup School retellings

situation: On-demand home cleaning marketplace. Connected customers to cleaners with the cleaners as the supply side. The cleaners had effectively no switching cost: once a customer and cleaner had connected through Homejoy, they could (and did) move the relationship off-platform to avoid the take rate.
action: Homejoy spent heavily on customer acquisition through deep first-clean discounts (often $19 first cleans at a real cost above that). They scaled aggressively into multiple cities before fixing the leakage problem on the supply side.
result: Customers used the discount, then booked the same cleaner directly. Cohorts didn't retain on Homejoy. Cash burned faster than retention improved. Homejoy shut down in 2015 and the team was acqui-hired by Google.
lesson: Two-sided marketplaces die from supply leakage. If supply has zero switching cost off-platform, no amount of paid demand acquisition will fix it — the platform is paying to introduce the two sides for free.
source: Re/code & TechCrunch coverage of Homejoy shutdown, 2015

situation: Prepared-meal delivery service shipping cooked meals to your door. Solid early traction in San Francisco. Raised heavily and used the cash to expand to LA, NYC, and Seattle before retention was solid in the home market.
action: Expanded to four cities, built kitchens in each, and added catering, breakfast, and frozen products at the same time. Pricing barely covered food + labor + delivery; founders assumed scale would fix unit economics.
result: New cities never reached SF's traction levels. Per-meal economics never crossed into profit. The company burned ~$125M and shut down in 2019, including refusing refunds to customers with active credits.
lesson: Geographic expansion before you have PMF and unit economics in the home market is the canonical premature-scale mistake. Fix the wedge before you replicate it.
source: TechCrunch & SFGate coverage of Munchery shutdown, 2019

situation: On-demand healthy meal delivery. Customers could order a fresh, prepared meal and have it delivered in 15 minutes. The model required commissaries (cooked food at scale), drivers, and packaging — all subsidised below the customer's price point during growth.
action: Sprig leaned into growth metrics — meals delivered, MAU, geographic expansion — while losing money on every order. Pricing did not change to reflect the true cost of the kitchen, packaging, and driver network.
result: Even after raising $57M+, the unit economics never inverted. Sprig shut down in 2017, citing the on-demand-delivery model as 'costly'. Founders publicly noted they had not solved the underlying economics.
lesson: If unit economics are negative, growth makes you lose more money. A growing top-line in an unprofitable per-unit model is not traction — it's a faster route to zero.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Sprig shutdown & founder retrospective, 2017
